text: Laparoscopic colorectal resection for a giant colonic diverticulum A giant colonic diverticulum is a rare diverticulosis complication with less than two hundred cases reported in the literature. The authors present a case of a sixty-two year old man with a painless hypogastric, left iliac fossa abdominal mass with several episodes of acute diverticulitis, proposed to a laparoscopic sigmoidectomy. As we can see at the CT scan, the patient presented a gas-filled mass with eleven centimeters in size, adjacent to the sigmoid colon and with close proximity to the bladder wall.

The patient is positioned supine for the laparoscopic procedure, utilizing four trocars: two 10mm trocars at the umbilicus and right lower quadrant, and two 5mm trocars at the left flank and upper umbilical midline. The surgeon inspects the abdominal cavity, revealing a large cyst adjacent to the sigmoid colon and bladder wall.

The surgeon identifies and ligates the lower mesenteric vessels near the trice angle. Dissection is performed anteriorly to the durotus fasciae, extending towards the pancreas and spleen. The splenic flexure is dissected anteriorly after opening the lesser sac and parietocolic ligaments.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the giant cyst, which is closely adhered to the bladder wall. An opening in the bladder wall is identified and sutured with PDS 4-0 sutures. Methylene blue is used to test the suture for leakage, confirming no leakage is present.
The surgeon performs a colonic section followed by a colorectal anastomosis, using a circular stapler to create a terminoterminal anastomosis.

The histological examination revealed a giant colonic diverticulum with bladder fistula and diverticular disease of the sigmoid colon. The patient had an uneventful post-operative period and was discharged after four days of hospital stay.
